claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, could you tell me:**
1. How many children do you have (or are responsible for)?
2. What are their names and ages?

This will help me assess whether they qualify as "qualifying children" for Child Benefit purposes. The eligibility criteria differ based on the child's age:
- Children under 16 may automatically qualify
- Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those aged 20 or over generally don't qualify

What are the details of the child(ren) you're asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I have one child, Alex, who's 8 years old. He doesn't live with me though - he lives with his dad.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with his dad.

Since Alex is under 16, he would generally be considered a qualifying child from an age perspective. However, there are some important questions I need to ask about your specific situation:

**Living arrangements are important for Child Benefit.** Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you may still be eligible in certain circumstances.

Let me ask you some questions:

1. **Are you responsible for Alex in any way?** For example:
   - Do you contribute to his upkeep/maintenance?
   - Do you have any parental responsibilities for him?
   - Does he spend any time living with you (even if not most of the time)?

2. **Is his dad currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** This is important because generally only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child.

Could you tell me more about your relationship with Alex and the current arrangements?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
I do contribute to Alex's upkeep - I help with things like his clothes and some other expenses. I give about £10 a week towards his costs.

And yes, his dad is already claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. This is an important situation to clarify.

**Key Issue: Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child.**

Since Alex's dad is already claiming Child Benefit for him, this creates what's called a "multiple claimant" situation. Even though you contribute to Alex's upkeep (which shows you have some financial responsibility for him), Child Benefit can only be paid to one person at a time.

**Here's what this means:**

In cases where more than one person could potentially claim for the same child, HMRC has a priority system to determine who should receive it. Generally, the person the child lives with has priority. Since Alex lives with his dad and his dad is already claiming, this affects your eligibility.

**Your options:**

1. **If you believe you should be the one claiming** (for example, if you're the main person responsible for Alex even though he doesn't live with you), you can still apply and HMRC will make a decision about who has the greater entitlement. This would likely mean his dad's claim would be reviewed.

2. **If his dad is the appropriate claimant** (which seems likely since Alex lives with him full-time), then you wouldn't be eligible while his dad is claiming.

**My question to you:** Do you want to dispute who should be claiming for Alex, or are you accepting that his dad should be the one receiving Child Benefit since Alex lives with him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not looking to dispute it. His dad has him full-time, so it makes sense that he's the one claiming. I just wanted to check if there was any way I could claim as well, but it sounds like that's not possible.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
You're absolutely right, and I appreciate you being clear about the situation.

**Final Assessment for Alex:**

Since Alex's dad is already claiming Child Benefit for him and Alex lives with his dad full-time, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex. As you've correctly understood, only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time, and the person the child lives with typically has priority.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

You are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- His father is already claiming Child Benefit for him
- Alex lives with his father full-time
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child
- You've confirmed you don't wish to dispute the existing claim

**What you should know:**

Even though you're contributing financially to Alex's upkeep (which is great!), this doesn't create a separate entitlement to Child Benefit. The benefit is designed to go to the main caregiver - in this case, his dad.

**If circumstances change:**

If Alex were to come and live with you in the future, or if his dad stops claiming Child Benefit, you could then make a claim yourself at that time.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and would normally be a qualifying child based on age (under 16). However, Alex's father is already claiming Child Benefit for him, and Alex lives with his father full-time. Under Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim for the same child at any given time. The claimant has confirmed they do not wish to dispute the existing claim, and it is appropriate that the parent with whom the child lives receives the benefit. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex while the father's claim remains in place.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 8), who lives full-time with his father. The father is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child, and the claimant does not wish to dispute the existing claim, they are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
